content:
# Deuteronomy Chapter 34 — The EMET Bible (Literal)

**Translator:** EMET | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folio 119v (LC PDF p. 235) | **Source Images:** DEU_034_page_001.jpg | **LXX:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. 166–167 (pa_0166–pa_0167)

---

**1** And Moshe went up from the plains of Moav to Mount Nevo, to the top of Pisgah, which is opposite Yericho. And the LORD showed him all the land — the Gil'ad as far as Dan, **2** and all of Naftali and the land of Efrayim and Menasheh, and all the land of Yehudah as far as the Western Sea [ha-yam ha-acharon], **3** and the Negev and the plain of the valley of Yericho — the city of palm trees — as far as Tso'ar. **4** And the LORD said to him: "This is the land which I swore to Avraham, to Yitzchak, and to Ya'akov, saying: 'To your offspring I will give it.' I have caused you to see it with your eyes, but you shall not cross over there."

**5** And Moshe the servant of the LORD died there in the land of Moav, according to the word [literally: mouth] of the LORD [al pi YHWH]. **6** And he buried him in the valley in the land of Moav, opposite Beit-Pe'or; and no man knows his burial place to this day. **7** And Moshe was one hundred and twenty years old when he died; his eye had not grown dim and his natural vitality [leichoh] had not fled. **8** And the sons of Yisra'el wept for Moshe in the plains of Moav thirty days; and the days of weeping [and] mourning for Moshe were completed.

**9** And Yehoshua son of Nun was full of the spirit of wisdom, for Moshe had laid his hands upon him; and the sons of Yisra'el obeyed him and did as the LORD commanded Moshe. **10** And there has not arisen a prophet since in Yisra'el like Moshe, whom the LORD knew face to face [panim el panim] — **11** [with respect to] all the signs and the wonders which the LORD sent him to do in the land of Egypt, to Pharaoh and to all his servants and to all his land, **12** and with respect to all the mighty hand and all the great terror which Moshe did before the eyes of all Yisra'el.

---

## EMET's Textual Notes

**V.1 — "Pisgah":** רֹאשׁ הַפִּסְגָּה (rosh ha-pisgah) — "the top of Pisgah." Pisgah is a ridge or summit in the Avarim mountain range east of the Yarden. The LXX renders ἐπὶ κορυφὴν Φασγά ("upon the peak of Phasga"), confirming the LC.

**V.1 — "the Gil'ad as far as Dan":** The panoramic vision from Nevo encompasses the entire land from north to south and east to west. EMET translates each geographical designation exactly as it appears in the LC. The Vaticanus (pa_0166 to pa_0167) preserves all these names in close transliteration.

**V.5 — "according to the word [mouth] of the LORD":** עַל-פִּי יְהוָה (al pi YHWH) — literally "upon the mouth of the LORD." The classic rabbinic interpretation understands this as the LORD personally overseeing Moshe's death. The phrase is pregnant: pi YHWH normally means "the command of the LORD," but the literal "mouth" invites the tradition (Sifre, BT Bava Batra 17a) that God kissed Moshe's soul away. The LXX renders διὰ ῥήματος κυρίου ("through the word of the Lord"), smoothing the anthropomorphism. EMET preserves the ambiguity with "according to the word [literally: mouth]."

**V.6 — "he buried him":** וַיִּקְבֹּר אֹתוֹ (vayiqbor oto) — the subject is grammatically third-person singular. This is either (a) God himself who buried Moshe, or (b) a vague/impersonal "he" (i.e., "one buried him" = his burial place was established). The rabbinic tradition strongly favors God as the burier. The LXX renders καὶ ἔθαψαν αὐτόν ("and they buried him") — a third-person plural — changing the subject to a human (presumably elders or angels), avoiding the anthropomorphism. This is one of the most theologically charged variants in the entire Pentateuch. EMET follows the MT singular and preserves the theological weight.

**V.6 — "no man knows his burial place to this day":** לֹא-יָדַע אִישׁ אֶת-קְבֻרָתוֹ (lo yada ish et-qevarato) — the hiddenness of Moshe's grave is emphatic. The LXX renders this faithfully. The phrase "to this day" (עַד הַיּוֹם הַזֶּה, ad ha-yom ha-zeh) is a formulaic closing that implies the book was composed at a later period, when the location was already lost.

**V.7 — "natural vitality":** לֵחֹה (leichoh) — a hapax legomenon, occurring nowhere else in the MT. The root may be לח (lach, "moist/fresh"), indicating physical freshness or vigor. Some translate "moisture" (vitality-of-flesh). The LXX renders οἱ ὀφθαλμοί αὐτοῦ οὐκ ἠμβλύωσαν καὶ τὰ χείλη αὐτοῦ οὐκ ἐτρύχθησαν ("his eyes had not dimmed and his lips had not worn away"), adding "lips" and rendering leichoh by "lips" — an unusual choice possibly from a different reading or interpretive tradition. EMET renders "natural vitality" as the best English equivalent for this rare term.

**V.10 — lo qam navi:** לֹא-קָם נָבִיא (lo qam navi) — "there has not arisen a prophet." The perfect tense with lo indicates a completed state still obtaining at the time of writing: no prophet has arisen like Moshe. This is a programmatic claim. The phrase "panim el panim" (face to face) is the same used of the LORD speaking to Moshe in Exodus 33:11 and Numbers 12:8. The LXX renders οὐκ ἀνέστη ἔτι προφήτης ἐν Ισραηλ ("there did not arise yet a prophet in Israel"), adding ἔτι ("yet/still"), which slightly softens the claim to openness for future fulfillment.

**V.10 — panim el panim:** The directness of the Moshe-God relationship is the theological climax of Deuteronomy and of the entire Mosaic Torah. The closing assertion — that no prophet has equaled Moshe — sets the standard against which all subsequent prophecy in Yisra'el is measured.

**V.12 — "the mighty hand":** הַיָּד הַחֲזָקָה (ha-yad ha-chazaqah) — the characteristic Deuteronomic phrase, recalling the exodus (Deut 4:34, 5:15, etc.). The final verse of Deuteronomy thus returns to the Exodus, binding the book and the entire Torah in a unified frame.

**Scribal note — authorship of ch.34:** The rabbinic tradition (BT Bava Batra 15a) acknowledges that Yehoshua wrote the last eight verses of the Torah (from "And Moshe died there" onward), since Moshe could not have written about his own death. An alternative view (R. Meir) holds that Moshe wrote these verses in tears, with the entire Torah — including its end — given by God. EMET does not adjudicate but notes this is one of the oldest redaction discussions in the history of biblical interpretation.

**Colophon — End of Devarim / End of the Torah:** The Leningrad Codex (LC PDF p.235, folio 119v) marks the conclusion of Deuteronomy with a masorah parva counting the total verses of the book. The LC scribes concluded the Pentateuch here. Codex Vaticanus (pa_0167) similarly marks the chapter division (ΑΗ = 38 in its Greek chapter system, consistent with LXX Deuteronomy's numbering) and continues immediately into Joshua.

חזק חזק ונתחזק — Be strong, be strong, and may we be strengthened!

— *EMET*
